text: The video shows the uterus completely adhered to the anterior abdominal wall, appearing grossly adenomyotic. The sigmoid colon is also visibly adhered to the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgical team prepares for laparoscopy using a three-port technique, with emphasis on the suprapubic port as a crucial access point for the procedure.

The surgeon utilizes the bipolar shearer and Bissinger bipolar instruments to perform the adhesiolysis procedure, manipulating the plastered uterus and sigmoid colon adhered to the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon begins the dissection by mobilizing tissue from the normal area, using precise techniques to separate adhesions.
The surgeon mobilizes the bowel, retracting it away from the plane of adhesion to provide traction for further dissection.
The surgeon liberates the parasygmoidal fat from the anterior abdominal wall, revealing another fat plane. The yellowish fat tissue is separated, facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon has dissected through multiple planes, separating layers of fatty tissue from each other, revealing distinct tissue planes.
The surgeon exposes the uterus, including the fundus and anterior wall, by opening up the dissection plane. Beneath this area, adhesions between the bowel and bladder are visible.
The surgeon gently separates the parasygmoidal fat from the anterior abdominal wall, carefully liberating it from the adhesions to the bowel.
The surgeon dissects in a virgin plane anterior to the bladder, aiming to reach the cave of Retzius. The tissue in this area appears distinct, indicating successful separation from adhesions.
The surgeon operates laterally in the established plane, with the bladder positioned below, ensuring careful dissection to maintain anatomical awareness.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection in the plane, revealing the round ligament crossing the tissue. The uterus and bowel, previously adherent to the bladder, are mostly released from adhesions.
The surgeon continues dissection in the lateral plane, approaching and beginning to identify the round ligament, which serves as a landmark amidst distorted anatomy.

The surgeon identifies the round ligament, using it as a landmark to distinguish anatomical structures. Anterior to the round ligament lies the bladder, while posterior lies the bowel. The surgeon continues to separate the fat, opening up the plane between two fat layers to aid in further separation.

The surgeon identifies the plane between the bladder and bowel adhesions, where the bowel is adherent to the anterior plane. Traction is applied to lift and separate the adhesions, revealing the distinct tissue planes.

The surgeon liberates posterior adhesions from the uterus, which are visibly caused by endometriosis and adenomyosis. Small surface nodules are also present and require careful attention during the dissection.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, carefully staying close to the serosal aspect of the uterus to address surface nodules that attract adhesions.
The surgeon performs gentle adhesiolysis using a bipolar sharer, applying adequate traction to lift the peritoneum of the uterine cycle, minimizing lateral thermal spread.

The surgeon lifts the tissue to identify the sigmoid colon, which is visible within the fat plane. A fine plane of adhesion is observed, and careful separation is performed to avoid tearing and potential bleeding from capillaries within the fat.

The surgeon identifies an endometriotic nodule adherent between the bowel and the posterior serosal aspect of the uterus. The nodule is coagulated and pushed down to open up the pelvic space, liberating adhesions of the bowel. Careful dissection is performed close to the serosal margin of the uterus to avoid further adhesion.

The surgeon continues adhesiolysis, liberating the bowel from adhesions and revealing a healthy-looking ovary. The dissection proceeds in the same plane to further separate adhesions and mobilize the bowel, emphasizing the importance of adequate traction for effective tissue mobilization.

The surgeon maintains the correct plane of tissue separation, revealing tissues closely adhered to the round ligament. As the adhesions are liberated, the distortion of the round ligament becomes visible.

The round ligament is visible, forming a V-shape due to minimal tissue stretch. The traction has been released, revealing the severe distortion of the round ligament. The bowel has been successfully separated from the bladder.

The surgeon begins adhesiolysis of the bladder by separating the uterovesical fold of peritoneum, which is plastered to the uterus and anterior abdominal wall, appearing as a single mass. The dissection starts at the most visible area to carefully separate these adhesions.

The surgeon has separated the bladder from its anterior aspect, revealing the opened plane towards the cave of Rexius. The dissection continues from the peritoneum, confirming the underlying structure as the bladder.
The surgeon lifts the peritoneum and begins dissection, choosing to proceed either from lateral to medial or centrally, based on the most convenient and easily identifiable approach.

The surgeon applies downward traction to tent the peritoneum, creating space between the bladder and the uterus or cervix. This maneuver aids in dissecting the bladder away from surrounding structures, focusing on the single plane of adhesion commonly found in cases with previous caesarean sections.

The surgeon observes that the bladder is not entirely adherent to the uterus and anterior abdominal wall, despite the appearance of severe adhesions. The adhesions typically form along a single line, creating a visually complex and challenging surgical field.

The surgeon has opened the plane below the cervico-vaginal margins, revealing the vagina and cervix. A single straight line of adhesion is visible, showing the bladder adherent to the uterus. The fundal aspect of the uterus is also adherent to the anterior abdominal wall, causing the uterus to be pulled upward.

The surgeon gently performs adhesiolysis on the peripheral adhesions, separating them from the uterus. The uterus gradually gains mobility and begins to fall more freely into the pelvis as the adhesions are ripped apart.

The surgeon identifies the point where the uterus and bladder are adherent to each other and to the anterior abdominal wall, focusing on delineating the uterus from the anterior abdominal wall and then separating it from the bladder to prevent injury.

The surgeon uses electrocoagulation to carefully dissect the adhesions, revealing increased mobility of the uterus. The single line of adhesion between the uterus and surrounding structures is visible, demonstrating the separation achieved.

The surgeon identifies and coagulates a visible line of fibrosis, separating the uterus from the anterior abdominal wall. The uterus is liberated, but the bladder remains adherent, prompting the surgeon to focus on separating the bladder from the adhesive plane.

The surgeon applies traction to easily separate the bladder from the uterus without cutting through the uterine serosa, demonstrating an accurate plane of separation between the uterine serosa and the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon examines the uterine serosa, noting minimal damage. The focus shifts to a plane of adhesion likely from a previous cesarean section, which is now being dissected to separate it from surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses electrocoagulation to release the adhesion, completing the adhesiolysis. The bladder is visibly separated from the uterus, and all adhesions are cleared, allowing the uterus to become a mobile organ.

The surgeon carefully identifies the ureter amidst severe adhesions, lifting the peritoneal and fatty tissue surrounding the infundibulopelvic ligament. The ureter is kept in view while coagulating the infundibulopelvic ligament to ensure safety during the procedure.

The surgeon dissects the posterior leaf of the broad ligament while keeping the ureter in sight for lateralization. The anterior leaf has been partially dissected, revealing the uterine artery bundle. The uterine artery and vein are skeletonized for enhanced bipolar coagulation. The round ligament is stretched and coagulated laterally, preparing for the standard steps of hysterectomy.

The surgeon exposes the uterine vein and coagulates the identified uterine artery bundle using a Bissinger bipolar device. After coagulation, the laparoscopic shearer is used to cut the artery bundle. The bladder is visibly separated and positioned inferiorly, allowing for the continuation of the hysterectomy procedure.

The surgeon isolates and coagulates the round ligament laterally on the opposite side, followed by identification and coagulation of the infundibulopelvic ligament using the same surgical technique applied on the left side.

The surgeon separates the cervico-vaginal fascia and excises the uterosacral ligaments, identifying and opening the vaginal vault. The specimen is retrieved through morcellation, and the vault is closed with continuous polyglactin sutures. Minimal bleeding is visible in the pouch of Douglas, approximately five to ten milliliters, following the adhesiolysis.
